result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen has been broken for eleven years. My mother never threw it out.

I think about that sometimes. How we keep things that no longer work, arrange them on shelves, dust around them, treat them with a kind of reverence usually reserved for the functional. Maybe we're superstitious. Maybe we believe that proximity to the broken thing will eventually fix it. Or maybe the real answer is simpler and harder: we don't throw it out because throwing it out means admitting something ended.

My grandmother kept her husband's chair at the kitchen table for six years after he died. Set a place for him at Christmas. My mother thought it was grief refusing to metabolize. I was twelve and thought it was the most honest thing I'd ever seen. Now I'm somewhere in between those two readings, which might be what growing up actually is — the slow migration from one misunderstanding toward a more complicated one.

---

I was on a train last March, somewhere between two cities I can't now remember, and there was a woman across the aisle reading a book with such complete absorption that the world around her had essentially ceased to exist. People boarded and departed. A child dropped a bag of chips and the chips scattered like small orange planets across the floor. A man argued briefly with his phone. None of it touched her. She turned pages with this quiet, absolute authority, like someone conducting a private ceremony.

I watched her for a long time. I'm not sure what I was hoping to understand.

I think I was envious. Not of the book, but of that quality of presence. The ability to be somewhere completely. I spend most of my time distributed across several places simultaneously — the room I'm in, the conversation I had three days ago, the task I should be doing, some imagined future version of an afternoon that will never arrive the way I picture it. Maybe three percent of me is ever fully present. The rest is elsewhere, managing, rehearsing, revising.

The woman got off at a stop I didn't catch. She tucked a receipt between the pages to mark her place.

A receipt. Not a proper bookmark. Just whatever was available.

I found that unbearably moving and I'm still not entirely sure why.

---

Here is something I believe: most of the important things that happen to a person are not dramatic. They don't arrive with music. They come as small moments that you almost miss — a specific quality of afternoon light, a sentence in a letter, the way someone laughs when they don't mean to. And you don't know they're important when they happen. You only find out later, sometimes years later, when you're doing something completely unrelated and the memory surfaces unexpectedly and you feel the weight of it settle in your chest and you think: *oh, that was one of them.*

This seems like a design flaw. It would be useful to know in the moment. To have some signal, some flag, so you could be more attentive, more fully there.

But maybe the not-knowing is the point. Maybe importance and awareness are incompatible. Maybe if you were watching for the moment, you'd ruin it, the way observing a particle changes its behavior. The significant things have to come at you sideways.

---

My friend Denise told me once that she thinks everyone has one question they spend their entire life trying to answer. Not consciously, not as a project, but structurally — the way a river has a direction. She said if you look at someone's choices over many years, you can sometimes identify the question underneath all of them.

I asked her what mine was.

She thought about it longer than I was comfortable with.

*Whether you're allowed to take up space*, she said.

I've been thinking about that for two years. Not because I think she's right, exactly, but because I can't fully argue that she's wrong.

---

The broken radio is white. Or it was white once. It's yellowed slightly the way old plastic does, taking on the color of time. There's a dial that doesn't turn anymore and a small speaker covered in a mesh grille and a crack along the bottom corner from when it fell off the counter probably fifteen years ago. It stopped working before it cracked. The crack is just a separate indignity.

My mother says she keeps it because she might fix it someday. She is not a person who fixes radios. She has never expressed any interest in fixing radios. But the *someday* is real to her — I can see it when she says it. She genuinely believes in this future version of herself who will learn what's necessary and restore the thing to working order.

I think that's actually beautiful. The persistence of the someday. The refusal to let the imagined future be cancelled by the repeated evidence of the present.

Or maybe that's just what hope looks like from the outside.

Maybe from the inside it doesn't feel like anything in particular. Maybe it just feels like a radio on a shelf. Something you pass every day, something you dust around, something you neither fix nor release.

Something that holds, in its broken silence, the memory of every song it ever played.
